title: Acrylic Craft Paint on Cotton
timeEstimate: 15 minutes while wet; pro-only once cured
stainChemistry: Craft / artist acrylic paint is a water-based acrylic polymer emulsion with pigment — the same basic chemistry as wall latex, but more concentrated and faster-drying. Craft acrylics (Apple Barrel, FolkArt, Liquitex Basics) usually cure fully in 10–20 minutes on fabric, tighter than wall paint's 15–30 minute window. Heavy body / artist-grade acrylics have more resin and cure into harder films. While wet, water-soluble. Once cured, the acrylic polymer coalesces into a flexible plastic film locked mechanically into cotton fibers. Like latex, the cure is irreversible at home temperatures. Heat-set acrylic is effectively permanent without solvent — and some craft acrylics are MARKETED as heat-settable fabric paint, which is the same chemistry intentionally made permanent.
whyThisWorks: Cold water + Dawn rinses the acrylic out while it's still emulsion. Fingertip work (not a brush) lifts pigment without driving it into the weave. Isopropyl alcohol softens partially-cured acrylic for a second cold-water cycle. Cotton tolerates extended soak which helps keep the paint re-suspended. The hard limit is the same as latex: fully cured acrylic is a plastic film, and home chemistry can't un-polymerize it.
defaultAssumption: Kid's craft project, school art, painter's shirt. Fresh splatter caught in the first 15 minutes = easy win. Discovered-dry acrylic, especially craft-grade, = often escalation or accept-as-is. Fabric paint (intentionally heat-set) = the paint is doing what it was designed to do and is permanent.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Scrape pooled paint with the edge of a plastic card, working outward from the center. Don't wipe. The clock is shorter than latex — craft acrylics cure in 10–20 min.
Turn the cotton inside out. Cold water through the BACK for 30–60 sec. Plug sink, fill cold, soak 5–10 min. Cold ONLY — heat cures acrylic permanently.
Work Dawn into the stain with fingertips while wet. No brush — bristles drive pigment in. Dwell 5–10 min wet.
Cold rinse from the back. Repeat Dawn + rinse while still wet if paint remains. Never let the fabric dry mid-cycle.
If paint started curing: dab 91% IPA on a white cloth, press on the stain to soften the partial film, blot, return to Dawn + cold rinse. Hidden-seam test if cotton is dyed.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: No IPA on silk/wool/rayon/acetate. Flammable — no open flame.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Air-dry flat. Inspect in daylight. Any ghost = no dryer, no iron — or the ghost becomes permanent. If still damp and ghost remains, one more Dawn + rinse cycle.

steps: 1. Scrape bulk paint with dull edge.
3. Dawn / NSD with fingertips, 5–10 min wet.
5. IPA on damp cloth for partially cured film.
6. Air-dry flat. Cured acrylic = amyl acetate (pro).
warnings: Craft acrylic wet window is shorter than wall latex — 10–20 min typical
NEVER dryer / iron until gone — permanent
Fabric paint is intentionally heat-set — designed permanent

homeSolutions.agent: Check — Craft Paint or Fabric Paint?
homeSolutions.instruction: Regular craft acrylic (Apple Barrel, FolkArt, Liquitex Basics) comes out of cotton IF you catch it wet — usually a 10–20 minute window, shorter than wall latex paint. FABRIC paint (Tulip, Jacquard, heat-setting craft paints) is designed to BE permanent on fabric once heat-set — if that's what was used and it's dried, it's doing what it was designed to do and there isn't a home path. If you're not sure which, assume craft acrylic and move fast.
Lift pooled paint with the edge of a plastic card, scraping outward from the center. Don't wipe — that drives pigment into the weave. Move fast; the cure clock is tight.
Turn the shirt inside out. Run COLD water through the BACK of the stain for 30–60 seconds. Plug the sink, fill with cold water, soak 5–10 minutes. Cold ONLY. Hot water cures acrylic permanently.
Keep the area wet. Work a few drops of Dawn into the stain with your fingertips. A brush drives pigment deeper — use fingertips. 5–10 minutes of dwell while still wet.

Rinse from the back with cold water until runoff is clear. Paint remaining? Repeat Dawn + rinse while still wet. Don't let the cotton dry between rounds — what's in the weave when it dries is locked in.

If the paint had started drying before you got to it, dab 91% isopropyl alcohol on a white cloth and press it on the spot. Softens the partial film. Blot, return to Dawn + cold rinse. Test hidden seam first on dyed cotton.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: No alcohol on silk, wool, rayon, or acetate. Keep away from open flame.

homeSolutions.instruction: Lay flat, air-dry. Inspect in daylight. Any remaining paint — even a faint ghost — locks permanently under dryer or iron heat. If you see a ghost while the cotton is still damp, one more Dawn + cold rinse round can sometimes finish.

Fully cured craft acrylic on cotton is a pro-solvent job (amyl acetate). Worth it for a nice shirt; not worth it for a kid's painting smock — sometimes "accept the spot" is the right call. Tell the pro: "Craft acrylic paint on cotton, [fresh / partial / cured], home-treated with cold water + Dawn + [IPA]. No heat applied. [Brand if known]." Fabric paint that was heat-set is permanent by design — don't spend money trying to remove it.

steps: 1. Craft acrylic or fabric paint? Fabric paint heat-set = permanent.
6. 91% IPA for partial cure. Hidden-seam test.
7. Air-dry flat. Inspect before any heat.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if paint is fully cured, was heat-set (including by dryer/iron), was fabric paint by design, or ghost remains after two cold-water cycles.
warnings: Cure window is SHORTER than wall latex — 10–20 min
Fabric paint is designed permanent — don't fight it

neverDo: Never hot water, dryer, iron — cures acrylic
Never brush — drives pigment into weave
Never IPA on silk / wool / rayon / acetate
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates Dawn, cold water, IPA, extended soak

materialWarnings: Wet window is tighter than wall latex — 10–20 min typical
Fabric paint is permanent by design

escalation.when: Paint fully cured, been through dryer/iron, fabric paint by design, or ghost after two cold-water rounds.
escalation.whatToTell: "Craft acrylic on cotton, [fresh / partial / cured / fabric paint], home-treated with cold water + Dawn + [IPA]. No heat. [Brand if known]."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner with amyl acetate

contextQuestions: Craft acrylic (Apple Barrel / FolkArt / Liquitex) or fabric paint (Tulip / Jacquard / heat-set)?
How long ago — still wet, tacky, or dry?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Fabric paint is acrylic chemistry engineered to BE permanent — no home path once heat-set. Heavy-body artist acrylic (Liquitex Heavy Body, Golden) has more resin and cures tighter than thin craft paint.
